title: Context Enrichment Window Around Retrieved Chunk
one_liner: After vector retrieval, fetch the N neighboring chunks before and after each hit (by stored chunk index) and stitch them into one overlap-deduplicated passage, so the LLM sees the surrounding context instead of an isolated fragment.
problem_solved: Small chunks embed and match well but read badly: the top-scoring chunk is often a mid-sentence fragment whose meaning depends on the sentences immediately before and after it. This technique keeps the precision of small-chunk vector search while returning a wider, coherent window of the original document around each hit, without changing what gets embedded.
failure_modes: The retrieved chunk is relevant but cut off mid-sentence or mid-argument, so the answer is incomplete or wrong
Answers miss facts that sit in the sentence right before or after the matched chunk
Small chunks were chosen for embedding precision, but they are too short for the LLM to answer from
Vector search returns isolated text fragments that lack the narrative or definitional context around them
Increasing chunk size to fix context problems degraded retrieval precision
when_to_use: Your documents are continuous prose (reports, articles, books, transcripts) where facts depend on surrounding sentences and sections
You deliberately use small chunks (a few hundred characters) for embedding precision but generation needs more context
Answers cite the right passage but are truncated or missing the qualifier that appears just outside the chunk boundary
You want better grounding context without re-embedding, extra LLM calls, or changing your chunking strategy
You control the chunking step and can tag each chunk with a sequential index in metadata
tradeoffs: Requires chunking-time cooperation: every chunk must carry a sequential index in metadata, and you must know the original chunk_size/chunk_overlap to deduplicate overlap correctly at stitch time
Larger returned passages consume more of the generation context window and can dilute relevance if neighbors are off-topic
Fixed window size is blind to topic boundaries: it may pull in unrelated adjacent text (Relevant Segment Extraction addresses this dynamically)
The notebook's neighbor lookup (get_chunk_by_index) does a full vectorstore scan per lookup, O(N) per neighbor; production needs a docstore keyed by index instead
The notebook also stores the full document text in every chunk's metadata, which bloats the index and should not be copied as-is
Not useful when chunks are already self-contained (FAQ entries, propositions, table rows) or when documents have no meaningful linear order (e.g. independent records)
how_it_works: At chunking time (prerequisite step), the document is split into fixed-size character chunks with overlap by a custom split_text_to_chunks_with_indices function, and each chunk's metadata records its chronological index. Chunks are embedded with OpenAIEmbeddings and stored in a FAISS vector store; a standard retriever with k=1 fetches the best-matching chunk. The core post-retrieval step, retrieve_with_context_overlap, takes each retrieved chunk's index, computes the range [index - num_neighbors, index + num_neighbors], fetches every chunk in that range via get_chunk_by_index (a metadata scan over the store), sorts them by index, and concatenates them while trimming the known chunk_overlap characters between adjacent chunks so the reconstructed passage reads as continuous original text. The notebook demonstrates the difference on a climate-change PDF and a synthetic AI-history document: baseline retrieval returns a truncated fragment about deep learning, while the enriched version returns the fragment plus the preceding and following paragraphs, making the answer self-contained. No LLM calls are involved; the technique spans chunking (index metadata) and post-retrieval (window expansion), with post-retrieval dominant.
key_dependencies: langchain (Document, FAISS vectorstore wrapper, retriever interface)
OpenAI embeddings via langchain OpenAIEmbeddings (requires OPENAI_API_KEY)
key_code_signals: split_text_to_chunks_with_indices(text, chunk_size, chunk_overlap) tagging metadata={'index': len(chunks)}
get_chunk_by_index(vectorstore, target_index) via similarity_search('', k=vectorstore.index.ntotal) metadata scan
side-by-side comparison of baseline retriever output vs enriched window output
related_techniques: relevant_segment_extraction: refines this idea by dynamically sizing the window from cluster of relevant chunks instead of a fixed neighbor count
semantic_chunking: alternative that prevents fragmented chunks at split time rather than repairing them after retrieval
contextual_chunk_headers: complementary; adds document/section-level context to the embedding, while this adds local neighbor context at read time
choose_chunk_size: prerequisite tuning; this technique lets you keep chunks small for precision and still return wide context
contextual_compression: opposite direction (shrinks retrieved context); can be applied after enrichment to trim irrelevant neighbor text
hierarchical_indices: alternative for long documents using summary-level then chunk-level retrieval
reranking: composes; rerank small chunks first, then expand only the winners
proposition_chunking: alternative when you want self-contained retrieval units instead of windowed reconstruction
summary: Context enrichment window fixes the classic small-chunk RAG failure: the top-scoring chunk is relevant but fragmentary, cut off mid-sentence, and missing the qualifying sentences around it. Instead of enlarging chunks (which hurts embedding precision), documents are split into small fixed-size overlapping chunks, each tagged with a sequential index in metadata. Retrieval runs normally against a FAISS index of OpenAI embeddings; then a post-retrieval step fetches the num_neighbors chunks before and after each hit by index, sorts them, and concatenates them while trimming the known chunk overlap, reconstructing a continuous passage of original text centered on the match. The LLM receives coherent, self-contained context while search stays precise. Costs are minimal, no extra LLM calls or re-embedding, but it needs chunking-time index metadata, known chunk_size/chunk_overlap values, and an efficient chunk-by-index lookup (the notebook's full-store scan is O(N) and should be replaced by a docstore). Best for continuous prose like reports and transcripts; unnecessary for self-contained units, and blind to topic boundaries compared to relevant segment extraction.
notes_for_chunking: Well-structured notebook, easy to chunk. One long overview markdown cell (cell-1) contains the entire motivation/method/benefits narrative and is the highest-value prose; it uses clean H2/H3 headers. Two image-only markdown cells (SVG diagrams, cells 2-3) can be dropped or replaced with alt text. Cells 4-9 are junk for retrieval purposes: pip install, git clone + sys.path boilerplate, wget data download (duplicated line), and a deepeval version warning output. The core content is three code cells with one-line markdown headers above each: split_text_to_chunks_with_indices (cell-14), get_chunk_by_index (cell-20), and retrieve_with_context_overlap (cell-24, the centerpiece, with full docstring). Keep each function with its preceding markdown header as one chunk. Cell-28 embeds a large synthetic document string (AI history) plus a demo; the string itself is filler and could be truncated, but the printed baseline-vs-enriched output comparison is illustrative and worth keeping. Final cell is a tracking-pixel markdown image (strip). Markdown/code ratio roughly 40/60; sections are self-contained; header quality good but some headers are verbose sentences rather than titles.
